mnemonics.title: KEDPPR = Six Requirements
mnemonics.content: Six requirements for public-key crypto: Key generation (easy), Encryption (easy), Decryption (easy), Private key derivation (infeasible), Plaintext recovery (infeasible), Reversibility (optional). Remember KEDPPR: 'Keep Every Door Protected, Privacy Required.' First 3 must be EASY, next 2 must be INFEASIBLE.

mnemonics.title: Easy = Polynomial, Infeasible = Exponential
mnemonics.content: Computational EASY means polynomial time: O(n²), O(n³), takes milliseconds-seconds. Computational INFEASIBLE means exponential time: O(2^n), takes millions of years. The GAP between these is what makes public-key crypto secure. Easy for good guys, impossible for bad guys.

mnemonics.title: Requirement 4 = Core Security
mnemonics.content: Requirement #4 (cannot derive private key from public key) is THE critical security requirement. Public key is published openly, so if attacker could compute private key from it, game over! RSA security: factoring n = p×q is infeasible. DH security: discrete log is infeasible. This ONE requirement protects everything.

mnemonics.title: Three EASY + Two INFEASIBLE = Asymmetry
mnemonics.content: The beauty of public-key crypto: three operations (key gen, encrypt, decrypt) are EASY for legitimate users, but two operations (derive private key, decrypt without key) are INFEASIBLE for attackers. This computational ASYMMETRY—easy one way, impossible the other—is the foundation. That's why it's called 'asymmetric' crypto!

mnemonics.title: Reversibility = Digital Signatures
mnemonics.content: Requirement #6 (optional reversibility): either key can encrypt, the other decrypts. This enables digital signatures! Encrypt with PRIVATE key (sign), decrypt with PUBLIC key (verify). Not all systems have this (DH doesn't), but RSA does. Remember: Reversibility = Signatures possible.

keyPoints: Public key cryptography requires six properties to be viable and secure.
Three operations must be computationally EASY: key generation, encryption, and decryption (polynomial time complexity).
Two operations must be computationally INFEASIBLE: deriving private key from public key, and recovering plaintext from ciphertext without private key (exponential time complexity).
Requirement #4 (cannot derive private key from public key) is the core security requirement; violation means total system compromise.
The computational gap between 'easy' (polynomial) and 'infeasible' (exponential) is what makes public key cryptography secure.
Optional requirement #6 (reversibility) enables digital signatures by allowing encryption with private key and verification with public key.
RSA meets all six requirements; Diffie-Hellman meets requirements for key exchange but not general encryption.
commonMistakes: Confusing 'computationally easy' with 'instant'; even easy operations take some time, but measured in milliseconds/seconds.
Believing 'computationally infeasible' means 'theoretically impossible'; it's possible in theory but impractical (millions of years).
Thinking all six requirements are equally important; requirement #4 (cannot derive private key) is the critical security requirement.
Assuming decryption should be as fast as encryption; decryption is typically slower but still 'easy' (polynomial time).
Forgetting that 'computational infeasibility' depends on key size; as computers improve, larger keys are needed.
Believing all public key systems support signatures; requirement #6 (reversibility) is optional, not all systems have it.
Mixing up requirements #4 and #5; #4 is about deriving the key itself, #5 is about decrypting without the key.
